content: The Black-Scholes-Merton model (1973) provides a theoretical price for European options. The formula requires five inputs: underlying price, strike price, time to expiration, risk-free rate, and volatility. Key assumptions include constant volatility, no dividends, continuous trading, and log-normal price distribution. The model revolutionized derivatives markets by providing a common pricing framework, earning Scholes and Merton the 1997 Nobel Prize (Black died in 1995). However, real markets violate assumptions—the volatility smile shows implied volatility varies by strike, contradicting constant volatility. Despite limitations, Black-Scholes remains foundational, with practitioners adjusting inputs or using alternative models for complex situations.
